title: There's a better way to print
instruction:
In Chapter 2, when you wanted to print a variable alongside text, you had to do something clunky like `"Hello, " + name + "!"`.

There is a much better way. **f-strings.**

Put an `f` before the opening quote. Then wrap any variable in `{}`. Python replaces it with the actual value.

`f"Hello, {name}!"` — Python replaces `{name}` with whatever name holds.

Run this and see how clean it looks.
starter:
name = "Alex"
age = 25
print(f"Hello, {name}!")
print(f"You are {age} years old.")
successMessage: Did you see that? No + signs, no str() calls, no quotation juggling. Just put the variable in curly braces. f-strings are one of Python's most-loved features.

title: Expressions inside f-strings
instruction:
Here is where f-strings get really powerful. You can put any Python expression inside the `{}`  — not just variable names.

`{2 + 2}` → 4
`{name.upper()}` → name in uppercase
`{price * 1.2}` → price with 20% added

Print the price with 20% tax by putting `price * 1.2` directly inside the curly braces.
starter:
price = 100
product = "Laptop"
print(f"Product: {product}")
# TODO: print price with 20% tax using an expression inside {}

codeChecks.message: Multiply price by 1.2 inside the f-string: {price * 1.2}
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Math inside curly braces. Method calls inside curly braces. Comparisons inside curly braces. The {} in f-strings accepts any valid Python expression — that is incredibly powerful.

title: Fix messy decimal places
instruction:
Floats can be unpredictable: `99.9` prints as `99.9`, but after some math you might get `99.99999999` or `100.00000001`.

f-strings let you control exactly how many decimal places to show:

`{price:.2f}` means 'show this float with 2 decimal places'.

Fix the three prices to show exactly 2 decimal places.
starter:
price1 = 9.9
price2 = 14.99999
price3 = 100.0

# TODO: print each price with exactly 2 decimal places
print(f"Item 1: {price1}")
print(f"Item 2: {price2}")
print(f"Item 3: {price3}")
successMessage: 9.90, 15.00, 100.00 — clean, consistent prices. :.2f means 'float, 2 decimal places'. Always use this for money. You cannot ship a price that says $14.99999.

title: Thousands separators
instruction:
Reading `8000000000` makes your brain work too hard. `8,000,000,000` is instant.

Add `:,` to format numbers with thousands separators:

`{population:,}` → 8,000,000,000

Add separators to all three big numbers below.
starter:
population = 8000000000
salary = 120000
distance = 384400

# TODO: print each with thousands separators
print(f"World population: {population}")
print(f"Annual salary: {salary}")
print(f"Distance to Moon: {distance} km")
successMessage: Much more readable! You can even combine them: {salary:,.2f} gives you $120,000.00 — number with separators AND two decimal places.

title: Column alignment
instruction:
For neatly aligned columns, you can set a minimum width and alignment direction:

`{value:<10}` — left-align in 10 characters
`{value:>10}` — right-align in 10 characters

Run the receipt below. Notice how < and > push the text and numbers to opposite sides. This is how reports, tables, and receipts are formatted.
starter:
items = [("Apple", 0.99), ("Banana", 0.49), ("Mango", 2.50)]

print(f"{'Item':<10} {'Price':>8}")
print("-" * 20)
for name, price in items:
    print(f"{name:<10} {price:>8.2f}")
successMessage: Perfect alignment — items on the left, prices on the right. < for left-align, > for right-align, the number is the minimum width. Real receipts, real reports, real data tables — this is how they are built.

title: print() has hidden powers
instruction:
`print()` has two optional superpowers that most beginners don't know about:

- `sep` — what to put **between** multiple values (default: a space)
- `end` — what to put at the **end** of the line (default: a newline)

Add two more print calls: one using `sep="-"`, one using `end=" | "`.
starter:
print("A", "B", "C")
print("A", "B", "C", sep=", ")
# TODO: print with sep="-" and then with end=" | "

codeChecks.message: Add a print with a custom end parameter.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: sep and end give you fine control over output. print("A", "B", "C", sep="-") gives A-B-C. Use end=" " to keep everything on one line.

title: Escape sequences — special characters
instruction:
Some characters need special treatment inside strings:

- `\n` — start a new line
- `\t` — add a tab
- `\\` — a literal backslash (important for Windows file paths!)
- `\"` — a quote inside a double-quoted string

Run the first three lines to see \n and \t in action. Then add a line that prints a Windows-style file path using `\\`.
starter:
print("Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3")
print("Name:\tAlex")
print("She said \"hello\"")
# TODO: print a Windows file path like C:\Users\Alex

codeChecks.message: Use \\\\ for a literal backslash in a file path.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: \n for newlines, \t for tabs, \\\\ for backslashes. These are called escape sequences and you will use \n constantly — it is how you add blank lines programmatically.

title: Raw strings — when backslashes get annoying
instruction:
File paths on Windows are a nightmare: `C:\Users\Alex` — all those backslashes need to be doubled.

Raw strings fix this. Put an `r` before the opening quote and every backslash is treated literally:

`r"C:\Users\Alex"` — no doubling needed!

Fix the broken file path by making it a raw string.
starter:
# BUG: \U and \A are not valid escape sequences — use a raw string
path = "C:\Users\Alex\Documents"
print(path)
successMessage: r"" raw strings — no escaping needed. Also essential for regular expressions, which are full of backslashes. Whenever you have lots of backslashes, reach for r"".

title: Multi-line strings
instruction:
Triple quotes `"""` let you write text that spans multiple lines without `\n` everywhere.

Perfect for long messages, email templates, SQL queries, or any block of text.

Run this letter. Then write your own multi-line message below it.
starter:
message = """Dear Alex,

Welcome to Python.
Things are about to get interesting.

Cheers,
The Python Team"""

print(message)
successMessage: Triple-quoted strings preserve everything — blank lines, indentation, all of it. They also double as docstrings for documenting functions. You will see them everywhere in production code.

title: .format() — the older style
instruction:
Before f-strings existed (they were added in Python 3.6), the standard was `.format()`:

`"Hello, {}!".format(name)`

Same result, more typing. You will see this in older code, so it's worth knowing.

Rewrite the two f-string lines below using `.format()` instead.
starter:
name = "Alex"
age = 25

# TODO: rewrite these as .format() calls
print(f"Hello, {name}!")
print(f"You are {age} years old.")

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: .format() still works perfectly. f-strings are faster and cleaner for new code — but when you see .format() in someone else's code, you'll know exactly what it does.

title: % formatting — the ancient style
instruction:
Before `.format()` there was `%` formatting — Python's original approach:

`"Hello, %s!" % name` — `%s` is a string placeholder
`"Age: %d" % age` — `%d` is an integer placeholder
`"Price: %.2f" % price` — `%.2f` is a float with 2 decimal places

Add a `%` formatted line for price using `%.2f`.
starter:
name = "Alex"
age = 25
price = 19.9

print("Hello, %s!" % name)
print("Age: %d" % age)
# TODO: print price using %.2f format

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: % formatting — still works, rarely written in new code. You will see it in legacy codebases. Now you can read it fluently. Use f-strings for everything you write yourself.

title: Build a receipt
instruction:
Final challenge — let's build something real.

Build a formatted receipt that looks like this:
```
Receipt
---------
Coffee         $3.50
Sandwich       $7.25
Total         $10.75
---------
Thank you!
```

Use f-strings with `:.2f` for prices and `:<` or `:>` for alignment. Make it look sharp.
hint: Use f"{item:<12} ${price:.2f}" for each item line.
successMessage: A real receipt with aligned columns and formatted prices. This is exactly how every POS system, invoice generator, and e-commerce confirmation email formats output. Chapter 4 done — you are absolutely flying.
